coredata.dc:title: Biogenic synthesis of N-doped carbon dots from S. cumini seeds for prostate cancer biomarker citrate detection, its live cancer cell imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Dongwei
Dai, Jindong
Jia, Jue
Kanagaraj, Thangamani
Rajalakshmi, Kanagaraj
Muthusamy, Selvaraj
Geng, Lina
Yuan, Guoyue
coredata.dc:description: Citrate is a potential biomarker for early stage detection of prostate cancer (PC), its concentration significantly dropped to 2–20 mM in PC patients. Herein, a cheap, simple, and reliable citrate sensor was proposed based on the biogenic synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) derived from the biowaste of Syzygiumcumini (S. cumini) seeds. The prepared N-CDs were characterized by TEM, FT-IR and spectral studies. The average size of the N-CDs was found to be 2.4 nm, the presence of –OH and –NH2 functional groups on the surface of N-CDs was confirmed by FT-IR analysis. The N-CDs possess the highest emission at 414 nm and cause quenching after reacting with citrate, which is due to the possible hydrogen bonding interactions between the probe and citrate. The probe expressed the lowest limit of detection of 3.5 nM, high selectivity, high interfering ability (1000-fold), provided a stable response at 5 min of reaction time, good biocompatibility, and delivered a contrast bioimage with different concentrations of citrate. The N-CDs were utilized to detect citrate in human urine samples, obtained good recovery results, and validated with the high-performance liquid chromatography method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Prostate cancer biomarker
Nitrogen doped carbon dots
Fluorescent bioimaging
Citrate sensor
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell culture, cytotoxicity and live cell imaging
Binding, quenching constants and quenching mechanism
Optimization of citrate detection using N-CDs
Detection of citrate in real samples using N-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterizations of N-CDs through (A) UV–vis spectrum, (B) FT-IR spectrum, (C) TEM images and (D) its size profile.

(A) UV– Vis spectra obtained for N-CDs in the presence of each 0.1 µM addition of citrate to the probe from ‘a’ to ‘l’, (B) Fluorescence emission spectra obtained for N-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of citrate (each 30 nM). Inset: The linearity plot obtained between the fluorescence intensity against the concentration of citrate.
Lifetime decay curves for N-CDs (red line) and N-CDs with citrate (black line).

Bar chart representation of selectivity and interference ability of TAG-CDs with possible interferences, 1 to 22 are: citrate, phosphate, malonate, succinate, fumarate, acetate, sulfite, oxalate, nitrite, nitrate, benzoate, glutamate, hydrogen peroxide, cadmium, zinc, mercury, lead, fluoride, ammonia, creatinine, urea and hippuric acid. For selectivity analysis 0.5 mM of each analyte was added separately to the probe solution, for interference analysis 0.5 mM of each analyte were added along with 0.5 µM of citrate.

(A) Bar chart representation of effect of pH on N-CDs in the absence (black) and presence (red) of 0.5 µM of citrate, (B) Plot of fluorescence emission intensity versus reaction time obtained for N-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of citrate.

Fluorescent confocal microscopy images obtained for (A) N-CDs-stained cells, N-CDs-stained cells treated with (B) 1 nM, (C) 2 nM, (D) 3 nM of citrate for 5 min and (E) its corresponding intensity profile plot.
Schematic representation of preparation of N-CDs form S. Cumini seeds, its sensing strategy with citrate.
Comparison of detection limit of citrate with earlier reports.
Determination of citrate in urine samples and validation with HPLC method.

head.ce:title: Biogenic synthesis of N-doped carbon dots from S. cumini seeds for prostate cancer biomarker citrate detection, its live cancer cell imaging

School of Medicine, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Clinical Laboratory, The First Affiliated Hospital of Hebei North University, Zhangjiakou, Hebei 075000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Citrate is a potential biomarker for early stage detection of prostate cancer (PC), its concentration significantly dropped to 2–20 mM in PC patients. Herein, a cheap, simple, and reliable citrate sensor was proposed based on the biogenic synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) derived from the biowaste of Syzygiumcumini (S. cumini) seeds. The prepared N-CDs were characterized by TEM, FT-IR and spectral studies. The average size of the N-CDs was found to be 2.4 nm, the presence of –OH and –NH2 functional groups on the surface of N-CDs was confirmed by FT-IR analysis. The N-CDs possess the highest emission at 414 nm and cause quenching after reacting with citrate, which is due to the possible hydrogen bonding interactions between the probe and citrate. The probe expressed the lowest limit of detection of 3.5 nM, high selectivity, high interfering ability (1000-fold), provided a stable response at 5 min of reaction time, good biocompatibility, and delivered a contrast bioimage with different concentrations of citrate. The N-CDs were utilized to detect citrate in human urine samples, obtained good recovery results, and validated with the high-performance liquid chromatography method.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Citrate is the first major intermediate in the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle; it plays a wide role in many biological activities [1]. Citrate is produced in mitochondria by the citrate synthase (CS) enzyme and transported to the cytosol or to the bloodstream cells. A small change in the concentration of citrate is linked with several dangerous diseases such as prostate cancer, kidney dysfunctions, nephrocalcinosis, and nephrolithiasis [2–5]. Citrate regulates the metabolic functions in immune cells like dendritic cells, macrophages, and endocrine cells of β cells. Accumulation of citrate is caused by the reprogramming of metabolic dendritic cells, which regulates the immune cells, fatty acid metabolisms, and citrate-linked acetylation of histones.The decreased level of citrate is related to the TCA cycle in tumor cells (Warburg effect). Citrate is central to regulating the metabolism of cancer cells, producing some enzymes (i.e., CS), transports (i.e., CiC), and its metabolizing (i.e., ACLY and ACO2) functions [1–5]. Mainly, the lower level of citrate in prostatic fluid is clinically considered an indicator of prostate cancer. The normal range of citrates is 50–200 mM in healthy males, whereas the prostate cancer-affected person has the value of 2–20 mM. The estimation of citrate levels in prostate or seminal fluids can be beneficial for determining early-stage risks of malignancy [6–9]. Additionally, it can serve as an indicator of the cancer's stage, location, and form. Further, monitoring citrate levels can be useful in other stages of cancer, including disease progression and observing malignancy regression. Hence, the simple, cost-effective, and accurate detection of citrate is very essential. Several analytical techniques are available for the detection of citrate, such as gas chromatography, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), capillary electrophoresis, spectrofluorimetry, spectrophotometry, electrochemical methods, etc [10–13]. Among them, the spectrofluorimetric method has its own advantages, such as cost-effectiveness, high sensitivity, non-invasive detection, ease of handling, and delivering the real-time spatial contrast images without damaging the target tissue or cells [14–17]. In our research group, the Schiff base chemosensor for the detection of zinc followed by citrate was studied [16]. Again, the non indicator displacement fluorescent sensor for citrate was established in 2018 using an aryl boronate derivatives material [15].

Recently, carbon dots (CDs) are receiving huge attention owing to their confined three-dimensional excitons, ease of preparation, low toxicity with high biocompatibility, high stability, and highly cost-effectiveness as derived from inexpensive raw materials [18,19]. Since CDs hold excellent water solubility and biocompatibility, the bioimaging applications were successfully evaluated. The high-nitrogen-containing triaminoguanidinium carbon dots were used for citrate detection, and its bioimaging was performed in live cancer cells [17]. Cheng et al. reported a citrate detection using g-C3N4 quantum dots and MgFe layered double hydroxide [18]. Zhu and his co-workers fluorescently visualized citrate using CdTe dots [19]. Aforementioned details encouraged us to improve in terms of the preparation of CDs in a cost-effective manner and the diagnosis of the prostate cancer via detection of the citrate biomarker.The biogenic synthesis of CDs is highly appreciated because it utilizes biowaste as a cost-effective raw material [20].

Mindful of the above thoughts, we have successfully synthesized N-CDs using S. cumini seeds and ethylene diamine (a nitrogen source). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on the N-CDs prepared from S. cumini seeds and used as a potential probe for the diagnosis of prostate cancer via detection of citrate. The present probe was well characterized by using TEM, FT-IR, and other spectral studies. The sensing environments were optimized in terms of selectivity, interfering ability, and reaction time. The present probe showed absorbance at 312 nm and its corresponding emission peak observed at 414 nm. After reacting with citrate, the emission intensity was quenched. Based on the obtained slope from the linearity plot of concentration versus intensity, the limit of detection (LOD) was found to be 3.5 nM. The probe showed 1000-fold interference tolerance ability and low cytotoxicity, which promotes live cell imaging in PC3 cell lines. The N-CDs were well penetrated to the cells and expressed contrasting images based on the concentration of citrate. The practical utility was further extended to evaluate the citrate level in urine samples, achieved good recoveries, and validated with the HPLC method of analysis.

1,2-Ethylenediamine, other solvents, and chemicals were purchased from Aladdin, China. Fetal bovine serum (FBS), Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) medium 1640, penicillin, Dulbecco's phosphate buffered saline (DBS), Hepes buffer solution, streptomycin, and other cell culture materials were obtained from Hyclone (USA). MTT-4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide was obtained from Beyotime, China. Benzoate, oxalate, acetate, succinate, sulfite, glutamate, fumarate, malonate, phosphate, fluoride, nitrate, lead (Pb2+), mercury (Hg2+),aluminum (Al3+),and cadmium (Cd2+) were obtained. All other reagents were of analytical grade unless stated elsewhere and used as received, excluding additional purifications. The deionized water (DI) was used in the whole experiments, which was self-processed in our laboratory purification system.

Ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer Cart 8454 (Agilent Tech. USA) was used to measure the absorbance spectra using a 1 mm path length of polystyrene cells. To recognize the functional groups available on the biogenic synthesized N-CDs, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was taken by Thermo Nicolet 380 FT-IR spectrophotometer. Fluorescence emission spectra were measured at Cary Eclipse (Agilent Tech. USA). JEOL Jem advanced analytical HR-TEM was utilized for carrying out TEM images of N-CDs. Using the HI 2210 pH meter (Hanna Instruments, Woonsocket, RI, USA), the pH of the solutions was adjusted. The TCSPC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc.) was used to analyze the fluorescence lifetime decay. A BioTek ELX800 microplate reader (Fisher Scientific, USA) was employed to analyze the cytotoxicity effect of N-CDs. Fluorescence live cell images were obtained using a Leica TCS SP 5 II (Leica Microsystems, USA) confocal laser scanning microscope with a 63 × oil immersion objective lens with a diode laser as the light source.

Syzygiumcumini seeds and 1,2-ethylenediamine were used as carbon and nitrogen sources for the biogenic synthesis of N-CDs (Scheme 1 ), respectively. Shortly, the seeds of Syzygiumcumini were collected as a waste product after eating the fruits, washed with DI water, and air dried at room temperature for about 10 days. The seeds were ground well into a fine powder material [20]. Then 3 gm of the seed powder was added into 25 ml of DI water along with 8 ml of 1,2-ethylenediamine with constant stirring. After mixing evenly, the mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and allowed to react for 8 h at 150 °C. Afterwards, the N-CDs were subjected to purification through centrifugation and dialysis. The reaction mixture was admissible for centrifuging at 10,000 rpm for about 5 min to remove the large particles. Later, a 3.5 KD pore-sized dialysis membrane was used to dialyze the above solution for 24 h. The obtained transparent brown liquid was collected, freeze dried, and the resultant powder was used for further required analysis.

The prepared N-CDs were diluted with DI water and used for all the spectroscopy measurements. For the UV–vis spectroscopy measurements, the concentration of N-CDs was kept as 0.4 mg/ml, and for emission spectroscopy measurements, the concentration of N-CDs was fixed to be 0.2 mg/ml. The desired concentrations of citrate and other interfering solutions were prepared using DI water. To verify the selectivity, each analyte was added to N-CDs separately and noted the fluorescence response. For interfering ability analysis, each analyte was added to the probe and a citrate containing mixture and noted the emission spectra.

The LOD was determined from the acquired emission intensities of fluorescence titrations. The fluorescence emission intensity was noticed for each concentration of citrate added into N-CDs about three times. The average of these three values was preceded by further calculations. Before that, the standard deviation of the blank for N-CDs was estimated by recording the fluorescence emission intensities of N-CDs in about 5 replicates without an analyte molecule. The linearity obtained from the plot average emission intensities obtained at each concentration of citrate added into the probe versus the concentration of analyte. From the obtained slope value, the LOD was calculated using the following equation:
LOD = -
3
σ b
m
where, ‘σb’is the standard deviation of the blank experiments, and ‘m’is the slope value obtained from the linearity plot.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture, cytotoxicity and live cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para:
Human prostate cancer cell lines (PC3) were cultured in ambient conditions such as a 5 % CO2 environment at 37 °C with RPMI 1640 medium containing 10 % FBS, 100 mg/mL penicillin, and streptomycin. The 96-well plate was used to culture the cells in the same culture medium for analyzing the cytotoxicity effects. For this, different concentrations of N-CDs (0, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 7, and 10 mg N-CDs dissolved in 10 ml of DI water) were added in cultured cells and incubated for about 24 h; further 4 h incubation was conducted with 0.5 mg/ml of MTT. The produced formazan crystals were dissolved in 0.1 ml DMSO and observed with a BioTek ELX800 microplate reader. The cytotoxic effect of a particular concentration of N-CDs was calculated by using the following formula, as reported.
Survival ratio (%) = (Absorbance of the sample/Absorbance of the control)*100

In the 35-mm culture dish, the cells were seeded, cultured, and allowed to stain with 100 µl of the diluted probe solution (0.2 mg/ml) for about 30 min and washed with DBS. These probe-stained cells were further incubated with 1, 2, and 3 µM of citrate for 10 min and subjected to fluorescence imaging.

The biogenic synthesis of N-CDs was done by using the biowaste of S. cumini seeds. Due to their large availability and high biocompatibility, we chose S. cumini seeds as a carbon source. The 1,2-ethylene diamine was used as a nitrogen source for the N-CDs preparation. The N-CDs were prepared by a simple one-step hydrothermal reaction at 150 °C for 8 h (Section 2.3, Scheme 1).

The synthesis of N-CDs was optimized by varying the reaction conditions, such as hydrothermal temperature and reaction time (Fig. S1). Initially, the absorbance of N-CDs was noticed at 312 nm, and following that, the emission profile was studied at different excitations from 280 to 320 nm. The best emission performance was observed at 310 nm excitation (Fig. S1B). Keeping this emission property, we optimized the synthesis of N-CDs by varying the reaction temperature and reaction time to achieve a good emitting behavior. It is clearly demonstrated that both reaction temperature and reaction time alter the emission behavior of resultant N-CDs (Fig. S1C, D). By increasing the reaction temperature from 120 °C to 150 °C, the emission intensity at 414 nm was increased with a slight red shift. Further increasing the reaction temperature to 160 °C, the emission intensity was decreased (Fig. S1C). Again, the reaction temperature was kept constant at 150 °C, and the role of reaction time was monitored. The N-CDs prepared at 8 h reaction time expressed better emitting behavior than 7 and 9 h reacted N-CDs. By noticing all the above results, we optimized the preparation conditions for good-emitting N-CDs, as the reaction time is 8 h and the reaction temperature is 150 °C.Using quinine sulphite as a reference, the quantum yield of the prepared N-CDs was found to be 0.58.

At the optimized reaction conditions, we prepared the N-CDs, and they were utilized for further characterizations, sensing, imaging, and other applications. The N-CDs showed an absorbance peak at 312 nm (Fig. 1 A); an emission peak was observed at 414 nm with a good stoke shift of 102 nm. The functional groups available on the surface of the biogenic N-CDs were analyzed by using FT-IR analysis (Fig. 1B). In the FT-IR spectrum, the strong peaks at 1025 cm−1, 1320 cm−1, 578 cm−1, 2310 cm−1, and 3330 cm−1 were observed. The peak at 1025 cm−1 corresponds to the OH functional moiety; the peak at 1320 cm−1 was attributed to the phenolic OH or NH bending vibrations. The characteristic carbonyl and hydride vibration peaks were observed at 578 cm−1 and 2310 cm−1, respectively material [20]. The strong and broad stretching peak was notified at 3330 cm−1 was due to the presence of OH and NH2 functional groups on the surface of the as-prepared biogenic N-CDs (Fig. 1B). Further, the size and morphology of the prepared N-CDs were analyzed by using TEM analysis. From Fig. 1C, it reveals that the N-CDs are spherical in shape with a uniform distribution without aggregation formation. The diameter of the N-CDs varied from 1.6 nm to 3.0 nm, with an average and maximum number of N-CDs of 2.4 nm in their sizes (Fig. 1D). Further, the presence of nitrogen atom was confirmed with elemental analysis, the results were shown in Table S1, it seems that nearly 7.5 % nitrogen presented in the N-CDs.

The major aim of the present study is to utilize the biogenically prepared biocompatible N-CDs for detection of citrate. The initial sensing ability of the prepared N-CD probe was tested by spectrophotometric titrations; the obtained results are shown in Fig. 2 A. For this, a 0.4 mg/ml solution of N-CDs in DI water was used as a probe solution. The N-CDs probe shows an absorbance peak at 312 nm (curve a). By adding a 0.1 µM solution of citrate, the absorbance peak intensity was increased with a slight red shift (curve b). Further adding each 0.1 µM of citrate, the absorbance peak intensity was increased with concurrent red shift up to 322 nm (curves “a” to “l’).The observed red shift was assumed to be the increased N-CD size during the interaction of N-CDs with citrate.

Further, the sensitive determination of citrate was achieved with 0.2 mg/ml N-CDs by using the spectrofluorimetric method (Fig. 2B). The N-CDs were shown an emission peak at 414 nm with an excitation of 310 nm (curve a). Upon adding each 30 nM of citrate, the emission intensity was decreased linearly without affecting its emission wavelength (curves “b” to “t”). A good linearity plot of emission intensity versus the concentration of citrate was obtained with the regression coefficient of 0.9802 (inset: Fig. 2B). Using the slope value obtained from the linearity plot, the limit of detection (LOD) was found to be 3.5 nM. The lowest LOD using the present N-CDs probe is really worth it when compared with the recent citrate sensor reports (Table 1 ). The biogenic N-CDs have OH and NH functional moieties on their surface (Fig. 1B). The negatively charged citrate anion may interact with the N-CDs through the possible electrostatic and hydrogen bonding interactions via the OH and NH surface functional groups present on the N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding, quenching constants and quenching mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
The observed quenching phenomenon was further studied through the Stern-Volmer plot and its binding quenching constants (Fig. S2). The binding constant for the observed quenching reaction at N-CDs with citrate was calculated using the following equation.
log
F 0
- F /
F 0
= log
K A
+ n log
Q
where “F0” and “F” are the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs and N-CDs reacted with different concentrations of citrate, respectively. “KA” is the binding constant, and “Q’ is the concentration of citrate. The linear plot obtained between log(F0-F/F0) versus log[Q] is depicted in Fig. S2A. In the linear equation, the intercept value is equal to the log KA and from the obtained intercept value, we found the binding constant value is 6.8 × 103 L mol−1. The quenching constant was calculated by a conventional Stern-Volmer plot. A non-linear plot of F0/F versus the concentration of citrate was obtained as shown in Fig. S2B. The obtained non-linear fit indicates that both dynamic and static quenching took place in the quenching reaction between N-CDs and citrate. The quenching constant was calculated from the Stern-Volmer equation as expressed below.
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
SV
Q
where “F0 ” and “F” are the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs before and after reacted with citrate, respectively. “KSV ” is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. Using the slope value obtained from the Stern-Volmer plot, the quenching constant was found to be 5.3 × 106 L mol−1. The obtained binding and quenching constant values demonstrated that N-CDs have strong interaction with the citrate. The quenching mechanism was further evaluated by time-resolved fluorescence lifetime decay analysis. The fluorescence lifetime was observed for N-CDs before and after reacting with citrate, and the obtained results were depicted in Fig. 3 . The average lifetime of N-CDs was 6.86 ns, and after reacting with citrate, the lifetime was observed to be 6.61 ns; the negligible decrease in the fluorescence lifetime decay indicates that the quenching of N-CDs with citrate follows a static quenching mechanism. The citrate molecule forms a non-fluorescent ground state complex with N-CDs via electrostatic interactions, which prohibit the fluorescent-emitting nature of N-CDs and cause quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of citrate detection using N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Our ultimate aim is to detect citrate selectively without affecting it by the other common physiological materials and conditions. The determination of any analyte molecule used to be affected by the possible interferences, pH, and reaction time. In order to obtain the accuracy of the sensing results, the detection conditions need to be evaluated. We investigated the selective responding and anti-interference ability of the N-CDs towards citrate, as shown in Fig. 4 . For this, the N-CDs probe solution was treated with 0.5 mM of each common interference, including carboxylates, cations, anions, and other possible analytes that are commonly present in body fluids.

Following that, the emission properties were investigated; it seems to be N-CDs could interact only with citrate. Again, the interference ability of N-CDs was also examined in the presence of other interferences in a 1000-fold excess; the emission intensity was not altered significantly. It indicates that the present system of N-CDs has good anti-interference ability and good selectivity towards citrate detection and would not be affected by the other interferences present in the biological system. Further, the influence of pH on the stability and binding of citrate with N-CDs were evaluated using N-CDs in different pH ranges from 4 to 11 (Fig. 5 A). The findings displayed that the probe N-CDs was stable in an acidic environment, whereas the emission of N-CDs was affected slightly in the basic conditions (pH = 8–11), but it is not up to the level of quenching caused by the citrate binding. Consequently, citrate (0.5 µM) was introduced to the probe with distinct pH ranges, and drastic emission change was observed at neutral pH (7). These findings strongly suggest that the probe can be used to monitor citrate in real samples as well as live cell imaging. The kinetic study of N-CDs was accomplished with different citrate dosages (0, 100, 200, 300, 400, and 500 nM), and as a result, the quenching process was vigorous and slowly attains a stable emission response in about 5 min (Fig. 5B). Hence, all emission experiments were performed following the incubation time of 5 min. From all the above experiments, it was clear that N-CDs showed selective response towards citrate, good emission, and were highly sensitive in physiological pH conditions, which permits N-CDs to be a more suitable bioimaging reagent.

All the above properties prompted us to explore its bioimaging properties towards detection of citrate in live cells using N-CDs. Before staining the probe in live cells, the cytotoxic effect was studied using conventional MTT assay analysis. The PC3 cells were grown on 96-well plate readers and incubated with different concentrations of N-CDs (0 to 10 mg/ml). The obtained cell survival ratio was plotted against the concentration of each probe solution and displayed in Fig. S3. The results reveal that the present probe N-CDs has low cytotoxicity effects; more than 85 % of cells were in live cells even treated with 10 mg/ml of the probe solution.

Further, the visualization of citrate in live cells was explored with PC3 cells. The grown PC3 cells were stained with 0.2 mg/ml of N-CDs probe solution for about 20 mins then the cells were washed with cell culture medium solution to remove the excess of the probe solution. Again, the stained cells were treated with different concentrations of citrate (1, 2, and 3 nM) for 5 min. Thereafter, confocal microscopy was employed to image the living cells, as depicted in Fig. 6 (A–D). The probe-stained cells displayed bright blue fluorescence (Fig. 6A), whereas the emission intensity was weakened on increasing the concentration of citrate (Fig. 6B–C). The cell images showed that the present probe, N-CDs, could provide concentration-dependent contrast fluorescent images towards citrate detection; it proves that N-CDs can be a good bioimaging reagent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of citrate in real samples using N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The probe’s practicability of the proposed sensing strategy was evaluated with a relative standard deviation based on the standard curve method. The urine samples were collected and diluted with DI water. By measuring the emission intensity, the added and found values were calculated and are shown in Table 2 . Interestingly, the results were in good alignment with the added and found values with the relative standard deviations of 1 %, and the obtained values were fairly matched with HPLC validation values. The ideal recoveries from 99.3 % to 100.07 % demonstrate the stability, practicability, and reliability of the present probe N-CDs towards citrate detection and its sensing strategies. The simple and effective sensing strategy using carbon dots were achieved [29–39]. The proposed biogenic synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon dots for cancer biomarker detection again giving a pathway in biomarker sensors.

In brief, a simple hydrothermal method was used to synthesize the probe N-CDs using the biowaste of S.cumini seeds. The prepared N-CDs were investigated through absorption and emission studies and characterized by TEM and FT-IR analysis. The present probe emits at 414 nm, in the due course the emission intensity was quenched with citrate. Based on the concentration dependent linearity plot, the detection limit was found to be 3.5 nM. The N-CDs showed high selectivity towards citrate, expressed 1000-fold high interference tolerance ability, provided a stable response in 5 min of reaction time and good response to sensing at physiological pH conditions. The present probe is highly biocompatible, which is proved by its lower cytotoxic values in high concentrated probe stained cells. The probe was successfully utilized as a bioimaging reagent in PC3 cell lines and delivered contrast images towards the visualization of the intracellular citrate. The good recovery results and fairly matched HPLC validation were observed with human urine sample analysis using this method.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 82202001 and 22150410327).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.125568.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Citrate metabolism of normal and malignant prostate epithelial cells
Urology

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.C.Costello, R.B. Franklin, Citrate metabolism of normal and malignant prostate epithelial cells. Urology 50 (1997) 3 – 12. Doi: 10.1016/S0090-4295(97)00124-6.
